This course is an introduction to the systematic study of literature. The selections focus on masterworks of literature from the 18th to the 21st century. There will be clusters covering selected regions: continental Europe, Great Britain, India, Africa, and North America. Students will learn how to read a literary text as an (a) artifact that (b) reflects the historical, social, and cultural circumstances of its production. Consequently, lectures will introduce basic concepts from critical theory and rhetorical analysis and apply these to the readings. We will also discuss, for each work, its historical background and how our knowledge of a text and its context can inform each other. Discussions and assignments are designed to sharpen critical skills and focus on practical exercises in interpretation.

This course looks at the origins, growth, and development, of college football from itŐs elite Ivy League school origins, to what is has become today: arguably one of the most popular sports in America. College football is steeped in rivalry, tradition, pageantry, and insane fandom. Games are literally played on almost every night of the week, and on Saturdays millions of people pile into stadiums that hold upwards of 105,000 people. College coaches make just as much as pro coaches, athletic directors are paid like NFL general managers, and the biggest college football programs generate in excess of $240M per year. However, there is also a dark side to college football: exploitation, academic misconduct, injuries, and scandal, which have been a constant thread in the sport since its founding in the late 1800s. This course will look at college football within the broader history of American history and culture.

This course looks at the origins, growth, and development, of college football from itŐs elite Ivy League school origins, to what is has become today: arguably one of the most popular sports in America. College football is steeped in rivalry, tradition, pageantry, and insane fandom. Games are literally played on almost every night of the week, and on Saturdays millions of people pile into stadiums that hold upwards of 105,000 people. College coaches make just as much as pro coaches, athletic directors are paid like NFL general managers, and the biggest college football programs generate in excess of $240M per year. However, there is also a dark side to college football: exploitation, academic misconduct, injuries, and scandal, which have been a constant thread in the sport since its founding in the late 1800s. This course will look at college football within the broader history of American history and culture.

This course looks at the origins, growth, and development, of college football from itŐs elite Ivy League school origins, to what is has become today: arguably one of the most popular sports in America. College football is steeped in rivalry, tradition, pageantry, and insane fandom. Games are literally played on almost every night of the week, and on Saturdays millions of people pile into stadiums that hold upwards of 105,000 people. College coaches make just as much as pro coaches, athletic directors are paid like NFL general managers, and the biggest college football programs generate in excess of $240M per year. However, there is also a dark side to college football: exploitation, academic misconduct, injuries, and scandal, which have been a constant thread in the sport since its founding in the late 1800s. This course will look at college football within the broader history of American history and culture.

This course is an introduction to the systematic study of literature. The selections focus on masterworks of literature from the 18th to the 21st century. There will be clusters covering selected regions: continental Europe, Great Britain, India, Africa, and North America. Students will learn how to read a literary text as an (a) artifact that (b) reflects the historical, social, and cultural circumstances of its production. Consequently, lectures will introduce basic concepts from critical theory and rhetorical analysis and apply these to the readings. We will also discuss, for each work, its historical background and how our knowledge of a text and its context can inform each other. Discussions and assignments are designed to sharpen critical skills and focus on practical exercises in interpretation.

This course looks at the origins, growth, and development, of college football from itŐs elite Ivy League school origins, to what is has become today: arguably one of the most popular sports in America. College football is steeped in rivalry, tradition, pageantry, and insane fandom. Games are literally played on almost every night of the week, and on Saturdays millions of people pile into stadiums that hold upwards of 105,000 people. College coaches make just as much as pro coaches, athletic directors are paid like NFL general managers, and the biggest college football programs generate in excess of $240M per year. However, there is also a dark side to college football: exploitation, academic misconduct, injuries, and scandal, which have been a constant thread in the sport since its founding in the late 1800s. This course will look at college football within the broader history of American history and culture.
